3. basic parser

Removing Useless Tokens

Section titled “Removing Useless Tokens”

We don’t need all tokens, so let’s filter some of them out. Implement a function in main.rs that will filter out:TokenKind::Tab, TokenKind::Comment, TokenKind::WhiteSpace.

⚠️ Implementation

What Is a Parser?

Section titled “What Is a Parser?”

A Parser turns ‘Tokens’ that it gets from a lexer into expressions, using Pratt parsing.

An expression is a more complex piece of information that is composed of other expressions

Example

25 + 55 * 40 will be turned into: Add { Number{25} , Multiply{ Number{55} , Number{40} } }

Expression can then be turned into other types of output. For example: assembly, code in other language.

Core Definitions

Section titled “Core Definitions”

Binding Power- BP

Section titled “Binding Power- BP”

Binding power says whether a specific token will ‘bind’ with other tokens:

Example binding power usage in code

Function / Expression Types

Section titled “Function / Expression Types”

Left Denoted -> Led

Section titled “Left Denoted -> Led”

It’s the function that is called when a token has a large enough binding power. It takes a current (left) expression, and current binding power.

Null Denoted -> Nod

Section titled “Null Denoted -> Nod”

It’s the function that is called for the first expression, in a tree ‘branch’, it doesn’t take any expressions as parameters.

Example

Section titled “Example”

Code to Parse

Section titled “Code to Parse”

25 + 55 * 22;

Token kindBinding power
Number0
+2
*3
;-1

Steps Needed to Parse It

Section titled “Steps Needed to Parse It”
  1. ‘expression()’ function from the previous example gets called for the first time, it has BP of 0. Reads a number token - 25.
  2. Nod function of the number 25 gets called, and we get: Expression::Number{value:25}
  3. BP of the plus sign is 2, so we will call its ‘led’ function with an argument of Expression::Number{value: 25}.
  4. Led function of the plus sign wants to get the expression on the right, so it calls the ‘expression()’ once again, with BP = 2
  5. It finds the number ‘55’, but it gets bound to the multiplication sign.This happens, because its BP is 3, and 3 > 2. Next we will call led function of multiply sign, with Expression::Number{55}.
  6. This function reads the next token - number 22
  7. The next token is a ’;’, which has a BP of -1, so it is ignored.
  8. Right side expression, for the plus-sign’s led function, will be: Multiply{ Number{55} , Number{40} }.

Output

Section titled “Output”

Add { Number{25} , Multiply{ Number{55} , Number{40} } }

Parser Struct

Section titled “Parser Struct”

We will implement struct named ‘Parser’ similarly to ‘Lexer’. It will be calling a function similar to the expression one from the example.

Properties

Section titled “Properties”
  • Current token index
  • Tokens
  • Valid datatype names: parsing c requires us to know names of all valid data types. For example: int,char,bool, struct, someSturctNameDefinedInCode.
    To do this we will have a ‘HashSet’ of all valid names.
  • File path: needed for debugging messages if you are parsing multiple files.
  • Token_stats: HashMap<TokenKind, TokenStats> this is a core variable that we will use throughout. We will be implementing ‘TokenStats’ soon.

Functions

Section titled “Functions”
  • Advance -> advances current token index.
  • Next -> next token without advancing.
  • Current -> current token.
  • Current_stats -> ‘TokenStats’ for the current token.
  • Expect -> returns a ‘result’ indicating whether the current token has the same kind as expected.
⚠️ Implementation

Expression

Section titled “Expression”

Now let’s implement ‘Expression’ enum. We will have to add a lot of types for it, but we will add them gradually when implementing a function to parse this type of enum.

Some of the expressions have other expressions inside them. When doing this we need to wrap them in a Box. Box is basically a pointer to a heap allocated memory. We need to do this so the compiler can calculate size of the data structure. Otherwise, it would encounter an infinite recursion. But with the pointer, size of the struct is always the same.

For now, add only those simple ones.

Token Stats

Section titled “Token Stats”

Token stats will allow us to, get the correct expression parsing function, for certain token type. Every stat will have: binding power, nod, and a led functions.

Note

The binding power was chosen so that:

  • Order of math operations is right
  • [ is always a led function
  • ’*’ is both dereference, and multiply sign, depending on if you use its led or nod function.

Your task will be filling the led, and nod functions so that they are parsed right. But more on that later.

For now I’ve filled out some basic functions that we will be implementing in a second.

Implementing the ‘Expression’ Function

Section titled “Implementing the ‘Expression’ Function”

Its described by this pseudocode, form the beginning of this page.

Your job is to make it work with the functions, that we have already implemented.

Caution

Remember about debugging information. This is a core function that we will be using throughout the parser. If we add some nice debugging information in here, it will make our life a lot simpler in the future.

⚠️ Implementation

Now that we have implemented it, we can write functions that will use this code.

Implementing Basic Parsing Functions

Section titled “Implementing Basic Parsing Functions”

Caution

Remember to put references to implemented functions, in the nod/led function,at the appropriate token stats. If you don’t know where to put it or if you have missed one token stat, don’t worry, you can always look at the code at the end of the “4. expression parsing functions” page.

Parsing_Functions::Prefix

Section titled “Parsing_Functions::Prefix”

It’s just a nod function for ’-’. (maybe also the ’+’). It is used to reverse the sign of its value: 5 + -5 = 0. It will be a nod function, that will be just advancing the parser. It will get its value using the ‘expression()‘

⚠️ implementation

Parsing_Functions::Open_Paren

Section titled “Parsing_Functions::Open_Paren”

Tip

Always use parser::expected(TokenKind::SMTH)?; when moving past a known token.

Example:

It’s just an expression wrapped in parentheses e.g. 5 * <u>(25 + 1)</u>.

It will be getting value using the ‘expression()’.

⚠️ implementation

Data_Parsing::Number

Section titled “Data_Parsing::Number”

Just an expression with parsed number. To parse number we could just use str::parse::<u32>(), but this doesn’t work with hex, bin, and oct values. So we need to implement a function that uses s.from_str_radix() for this.

⚠️ implementation

Parsing_Functions::Binary

Section titled “Parsing_Functions::Binary”

Binary expression will be used for most of the math operations: + - * / % << >> >= > < <=

It is really simple, it has to store: expression on the left, and right, and operator. Make it work like the example from the beginning

⚠️ implementation

Using Implemented Code

Section titled “Using Implemented Code”

Parse Function

Section titled “Parse Function”

Now we need to use all the, code that we have implemented on this page. Create a ‘parse’ function in parser/mod.rs:

  1. Initialize ‘Parser’, for the valid_data_type_names. Use standard c data types like: char, int, long, etc.
  2. Do this until you encounter TokenKind::EndOfFile:
    • Use ‘parsing_functions::expression(&mut parser, 0)’, and store outputs inside of a vector.
    • If you encounter a semicolon just continue the loop
  3. output all parsed expressions
⚠️ implementation

You might need to import a few things to make this work…

Running Code

Section titled “Running Code”

Temporarily replace your test.c file with a simpler test code like this:

Run the project with cargo run. You should see beautiful output, of parsed expression in the console. Please make sure that expressions were parsed in the right way.

Bugs

Section titled “Bugs”

If you find anything to improve in this project’s code, please create an issue describing it on the GitHub repository for this project. For website-related issues, create an issue here.

Support

Section titled “Support”

All pages on this site are written by a human, and you can access everything for free without ads. If you find this work valuable, please give a star to the GitHub repository for this project.
